retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Stones -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous suprapubic cystolitholapaxy is the preferred treatment for pediatric bladder stones, especially in males aged 16 or younger, to minimize urethral trauma. [69] Larger caliber instruments can be placed suprapubically rather than transurethrally, which is particularly advantageous in pediatric patients. A combined procedure using both suprapubic and cystoscopic approaches may be used in some cases. In adult male patients with outlet obstruction, transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) or a similar procedure is recommended, when indicated, immediately after the stone has been fragmented and removed. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Kidney -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the event a patient develops renal calculi in the ectopic, pelvic kidney, operative intervention is much more complicated than in an anatomically normal renal unit due to the different location of the ureters. [24] Laparoscopic-assisted percutaneous transperitoneal nephrolithotomy has been used to successfully treat stones too large for shockwave lithotripsy, which is more effective in renal stones under 2 cm in diameter. This procedure may require the mobilization of the colon, and there is a risk of urine leak into the abdomen, but it is an option if the stone is not contained within the renal pelvis and calyces. [25] Mobilization of the bowel can be avoided with a transmesocolic pyelolithotomy or with robot-assisted surgery, which has the added benefit of being able to remain within the retroperitoneal space reducing the risk of abdominal urine leak and urinoma formation. [26] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Kidney -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In general, significant surgery is discouraged in ectopic kidneys except for specific problems, and most cases can be managed conservatively. Percutaneous access to relieve an obstruction or for percutaneous nephrolithotomy may be more complicated or even impossible in some cases depending on the individual anatomy, and this needs to be taken into consideration. 24-hour urine testing for nephrolithiasis prophylaxis is recommended in patients that have had nephrolithiasis with ectopic kidneys due to the increased complexity of kidney stone surgery in these individuals and the risk of loss of renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Kidney -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team approach is needed to do this effectively with good communication between the diagnosing clinician and, if needed, pediatric urologists in antenatally diagnosed cases who would continue care after delivery. In the case of a postnatal diagnosis, the clinician would also need to arrange appropriate follow up. Urology should be involved early to help with diagnosing specific problems associated with ectopic pelvic kidneys and to correct them if necessary. This is of particular importance if the patient develops long term complications such as chronic kidney disease or stones. Patients need to be empowered to take ownership of their care and fully understand their condition and treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Stones -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL) can be equally effective for bladder stones, but the treatment head must be positioned above the patient (or the patient placed in a prone position) and may require multiple treatment sessions (up to 3) to achieve comparable results. [60] [61] [62] [63] [64] [65] [66] An advantage of ESWL is that it is the least invasive surgical option. [61] [67] ESWL may also be used in children with bladder calculi. [60] [68] Stones impacted in the urethra can be repositioned into the bladder and treated with ESWL. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Struvite and Triple Phosphate Renal Calculi -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Rarely, if PCNL is not feasible due to anatomic abnormalities like pelvic kidney, spinal deformities, or retro-renal colon, open surgery like atrophic nephrolithotomy might be considered. Surgery should also be considered in the morbidly obese patient, in whom endoscopy and fluoroscopy are difficult. The acute complications rate is lowest for PCNL and highest for open surgery and SWL. Some of the common acute complications associated with stone removal procedures are perforation of the renal pelvis, perinephric hematoma, significant blood loss, hydrothorax, pneumothorax, sepsis, renal impairment, and wound infection, among others. Long-term complications include stone recurrence, renal impairment, and growth of residual stone fragments. Antibiotics should be continued postoperatively. However, there are no current guidelines regarding specific regimen, dose, or duration.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Tips, advice, and suggestions during ESWL therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Prone positioning may be necessary to target a stone located over the iliac crest if the lithotripter's treatment head cannot be rotated adequately. Treating the same stone with more than 2 ESWL procedures does not appear to be beneficial. Ureteroscopy should be used instead if 2 ESWL procedures on the same stone have failed. Postoperatively, the use of α-blocker medications helps stone fragments pass and improves ESWL's overall success rate. For further details and information on ESWL, see StatPearls' companion reference, " Extracorporeal Shockwave Lithotripsy ." ESWL's contraindications include the following: An active or untreated urinary tract infection Distal ureteral obstruction Pregnancy Treatment path obstructed by an aneurysm, tumor, or other pathology that may be injured Uncorrected coagulation disorder UPJ obstruction, which applies to renal calculi

retrieved_docs.title: Struvite and Triple Phosphate Renal Calculi -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy: Shock-wave lithotripsy (SWL) should be considered only when the stone size is less than 2.5 cm with a non-dilated collecting system, or in the pediatric population. [35] When SWL is used, either a double J stent or percutaneous nephrostomy tube should be placed before treatment initiation to ensure adequate drainage. A percutaneous nephrostomy is necessary for adequate renal drainage in up to 40% of cases. [36] At least 50% of staghorn stone patients treated with SWL will require at least one more surgical treatment to become stone-free. Patients with wide infundibulae that allow easier transit of small stones are better candidates for SWL treatment than similar patients with narrow infundibulae, which will limit or restrict the passage of small stone fragments. Patients should be informed that multiple procedures may be needed to make them stone-free. Overall stone-free rates for staghorn patients treated with SWL alone is about 60%, but in those with smaller stones, this rate goes up to 90%.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- ESWL advantages and disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: ESWL is significantly less invasive than ureteroscopy, with a lower complication rate, inherent safety, and comparable stone-free rate, though it sometimes requires a second procedure. Minimal anesthesia, typically IV sedation, is needed for ESWL, with short and predictable surgical times. ESWL is suitable for stones anywhere in the ureters or kidneys, though this procedure is best for upper urinary tract stones. ESWL does not require pre-stenting unless necessary for other reasons, such as symptom relief or infections. The procedure may be used in pediatrics and older individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Urolithiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Renal ultrasound is a method that can be used to assess urolithiasis and is an ideal initial imaging study of choice in pediatric and pregnant patients to avoid radiation. [18] This form of imaging will identify stones within the kidneys, pyeloureteric, and vesicoureteric junctions, and identify hydronephrosis secondary to obstructive urolithiasis. Doppler jet can also be used to assess urinary flow. The sensitivity and specificity of ureteric stones are 57% and 97.5%. Stones will appear echogenic (bright white) on ultrasound. A large body habitus can significantly limit the visualization of stones. Assessment of the size of the stone can also be operator-dependent.

retrieved_docs.title: Urolithiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with large stones, or if the presentation is consistent with acute renal failure, oliguria/anuria, SIRS criteria, associated infection, or a history of the solitary kidney is present, may require urgent/emergent urologic intervention. Intractable pain or vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, pregnancy, or pediatric patients may require hospitalization for closer observation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Kidney -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pancake kidneys, like other ectopic kidneys, are usually asymptomatic but are also associated with an increased risk of recurrent urinary tract infection and stone formation. [17] This increased risk of nephrolithiasis in renal ectopia is due to reduced urinary flow through a more tortuous ureter and the vascular anomalies that are associated with the condition. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Calculi, Nephrolithiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The health care team involved in the care of patients with nephrolithiasis has an absolute obligation to inform patients on preventative dietary changes and how to optimize urine chemistries, particularly in cases of multiple stone recurrences, solitary kidneys, high surgical risk factors, or those in the pediatric age group. [159] [160]

retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). These nonspecific genitourinary manifestations frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (kidney stone), indicating that patients presenting with such symptoms should be evaluated for possible calculi within the urinary system. Recognizing that genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions belong to the broader umbrella of urinary tract disorders and are often associated with kidney stones helps clinicians prioritize diagnostic testing and management strategies aimed at identifying and treating underlying stone disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Other operations on urinary tract is a procedural category that encompasses several specific child interventions, including Replacement of ureterostomy tube and تفتيت الحصوات بالموجات الصدمية خارج الجسم. Both of these procedures are considered subtypes within the broader group of urinary tract surgeries, highlighting the range of therapeutic options available for managing ureteral stoma maintenance and extracorporeal shock‑wave fragmentation of urinary stones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrolithotomy for removal of calculus (procedure) is a surgical intervention designed to extract kidney stones and it incorporates nephrotomy and nephrostomy as subordinate techniques. Nephrotomy refers to an incision into the renal parenchyma to directly access and remove the calculus, while nephrostomy involves creating a percutaneous tract for drainage or instrument passage. By including these child procedures, nephrolithotomy provides a comprehensive approach to stone removal and postoperative renal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrolithotomy for removal of calculus (procedure) is a surgical intervention specifically associated with the management of calculus of urinary tract, indicating its primary role in treating urinary stones. Within this overarching procedure, the techniques of nephrotomy and nephrostomy are considered child components, reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which these sub‑procedures are employed as part of the nephrolithotomy approach to access, fragment, and drain the urinary tract calculi. Understanding this procedural linkage aids clinicians in selecting appropriate operative strategies for effective stone removal and postoperative care.

This specific method is considered a child procedure within the broader category of other urinary‑tract operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions are noted to co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (seminal vesicle stone) and with فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia). This overlap suggests that patients who present with nonspecific or poorly defined genitourinary complaints may also have underlying seminal vesicle calculi or benign prostatic enlargement. Recognizing the frequent association among these entities can prompt clinicians to consider targeted imaging or urological evaluation, leading to more accurate diagnosis and appropriate management of the underlying stone disease or prostatic hyperplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on the urethra are performed directly on the uretra, which serves as the anatomical site where clinical findings such as urethral discharge (تدلّي الإحليل) and urethral obstruction (اِنسِداد الإحليل) are observed. Cystourethroscopy with internal male urethrotomy is a specific type of urethral procedure, illustrating how endoscopic techniques are applied to the uretra. Incision and exploration of the urethra is categorized both as a child of procedures on the urethra and as a component of broader urinary system examinations, highlighting its dual role in diagnostic and therapeutic urologic care. Lithotomy, a general surgical approach for stone removal, includes the more specialized removal of a calculus of the urethra with incision; this procedure focuses on the treatment of a urethral stone (حصاة الإحليل) and is also classified under procedures on the urethra. The clinical entity “urinary tract disorder” (اضطراب السبيل البولي) encompasses a range of urethral conditions, including urethral discharge as a broader manifestation and urethral stone as a narrower, specific manifestation. Together, these relationships delineate how anatomical sites, diagnostic findings, and procedural categories interconnect within urologic practice, informing comprehensive patient assessment and targeted therapeutic interventions.
